question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker (front-runner) who observes a signed Permit2 PermitTransferFrom in the mempool submit it via a direct call to Permit2.permitTransferFrom before MidnightBundles.buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral executes, consuming the nonce and causing the bundler call to revert with InvalidNonce, under preconditions where the user has approved Permit2 and signed a PermitTransferFrom with nonce=N for loanToken, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls Permit2.permitTransferFrom(permit, SignatureTransferDetails(attacker, amount), owner, sig)] then [user calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with same permit], violating the invariant that a valid signed Permit2 authorization must be consumable exactly once by the intended bundler call, causing scoped impact: fund freeze (user's bundler flow is DoS'd, collateral supply or borrow is blocked)? Proof idea: fuzz nonce values and front-run timing; assert that after attacker consumes nonce, bundler call reverts with InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker replay a Permit2 signature originally signed for buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral (loanToken, amount=X) in a second call to repayAndWithdrawCollateral (same loanToken, same amount=X) under preconditions where the user signed PermitTransferFrom(token=loanToken, amount=X, nonce=N, deadline=T) and the nonce N has not yet been consumed, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls repayAndWithdrawCollateral with the same permit data, taker=msg.sender, onBehalf=msg.sender], violating the invariant that a Permit2 signature must only authorize the specific bundler operation it was intended for, causing scoped impact: unauthorized seizure (user's loan tokens are pulled and used to repay a debt they did not intend to repay in that call)? Proof idea: unit test constructing two different bundler calls with identical Permit2 permit data and assert the second call succeeds when it should not.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker replay a Permit2 signature signed for supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget (collateralToken, amount=C) in a call to supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget (same collateralToken, same amount=C) under preconditions where the user signed PermitTransferFrom(token=collateralToken, amount=C, nonce=N, deadline=T) and nonce N is unused, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls supplyCollateralAndSellWithAssetsTarget with the same CollateralSupply permit, taker=victim, receiver=attacker], violating the invariant that a Permit2 signature must not be reusable across different bundler entry-points with different economic outcomes, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption (collateral is supplied and sold under different terms than the user intended)? Proof idea: differential test comparing outcomes of both bundler functions with identical permit data.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit Permit2's unordered nonce bitmap to pre-invalidate a victim's nonce by calling Permit2.invalidateUnorderedNonces(wordPos, mask) directly, under preconditions where the victim has signed a PermitTransferFrom with nonce=N (wordPos = N >> 8, bitPos = N & 0xff) and has not yet submitted the bundler transaction, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls Permit2.invalidateUnorderedNonces(N >> 8, 1 << (N & 0xff)) as victim's address is not required], violating the invariant that only the token owner can invalidate their own Permit2 nonces, causing scoped impact: fund freeze (victim's signed permit is permanently invalidated, bundler call reverts)? Proof idea: fuzz nonce values; assert that after invalidateUnorderedNonces, the victim's bundler call reverts with InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a nonce desync by submitting a victim's valid Permit2 signature (nonce=N) via a direct Permit2.permitTransferFrom call with SignatureTransferDetails(to=attacker, requestedAmount=amount), under preconditions where the victim signed for spender=MidnightBundles but the Permit2 hash includes msg.sender as spender (not a parameter), triggering call sequence: [attacker calls Permit2.permitTransferFrom directly with victim's sig, to=attacker], violating the invariant that Permit2 signatures must only be consumable by the designated spender (MidnightBundles), causing scoped impact: direct loss (victim's tokens are transferred to attacker)? Proof idea: unit test calling Permit2.permitTransferFrom directly from attacker address with victim's sig; assert transfer fails because spender mismatch in hash.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that IPermit2 interface in MidnightBundles does not expose nonceBitmap, causing the bundler to blindly call permitTransferFrom without pre-checking nonce validity, under preconditions where a victim's nonce has been consumed by a prior transaction (legitimate or griefed), triggering call sequence: [victim calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with stale nonce in permit data], violating the invariant that the bundler must not silently fail on invalid permit data in a way that leaves the user's state partially updated, causing scoped impact: fund freeze (bundler reverts mid-execution after partial state changes if any occurred before pullToken)? Proof idea: state-transition test; call bundler with already-consumed nonce and assert revert propagates cleanly with no partial state.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the deadline edge case where block.timestamp == permit.deadline (Permit2 uses strict greater-than: block.timestamp > deadline reverts, so equality is valid) to replay a permit in the same block as its expiry, under preconditions where a victim signed a PermitTransferFrom with deadline=T and the attacker observes the signature at block.timestamp=T, triggering call sequence: [attacker front-runs in the same block with deadline=T, calls bundler or Permit2 directly], violating the invariant that an expired permit must not be consumable, causing scoped impact: unauthorized seizure (victim's tokens are pulled in the last valid block when victim may have assumed the permit expired)? Proof idea: fuzz deadline values at boundary block.timestamp == deadline; assert permit is accepted.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the non-standard EIP-712 domain in EcrecoverAuthorizer (EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract) — missing name and version fields) to cause hardware wallets or EIP-712-aware signers to display an unrecognized or misleading signing prompt, under preconditions where a user is asked to sign an Authorization struct via EcrecoverAuthorizer, triggering call sequence: [attacker presents a signing request using the non-standard domain; user signs believing it is a standard EIP-712 message], violating the invariant that EIP-712 domain separators must unambiguously identify the signing context to prevent user confusion, causing scoped impact: gate bypass (user unknowingly authorizes an operator they did not intend to authorize)? Proof idea: differential test comparing EcrecoverAuthorizer domain hash against standard EIP712Domain(string name,string version,uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract); assert they differ and document wallet display behavior.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker who is already authorized by Alice (isAuthorized[Alice][attacker] == true) sign a new Authorization struct on Alice's behalf to authorize a third address Charlie (or themselves again after revocation), under preconditions where Alice previously called midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, Alice) and EcrecoverAuthorizer is authorized by Alice, triggering call sequence: [attacker signs Authorization(authorizer=Alice, authorized=Charlie, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N, deadline=T); anyone calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig)], violating the invariant that only the authorizer themselves should be able to grant new authorizations via signature, causing scoped impact: gate bypass (attacker escalates privileges by authorizing additional addresses on Alice's behalf)? Proof idea: unit test where attacker is authorized, then signs and submits an Authorization for a new address; assert midnight.isAuthorized(Alice, Charlie) becomes true.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker grief a victim by front-running a valid Authorization signature submission (nonce=N) to consume the nonce before the victim's intended transaction, under preconditions where the victim broadcast a signed Authorization with nonce=N and the attacker observes it in the mempool, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) before victim's transaction], violating the invariant that a signed authorization must be submittable by the intended caller without front-run interference, causing scoped impact: fund freeze (victim's authorization flow is disrupted; their nonce advances, invalidating any other pending signed messages with nonce=N)? Proof idea: simulate front-run in test; assert victim's subsequent call with nonce=N reverts with InvalidNonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the permissionless nature of EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized (no msg.sender check) to submit a victim's valid revocation signature (isAuthorized=false) at a strategically chosen time, under preconditions where the victim signed an Authorization(isAuthorized=false, nonce=N) to revoke an operator but has not yet submitted it, triggering call sequence: [attacker submits the revocation signature at a time when the victim needs the operator to be active (e.g., during a liquidation they are trying to avoid)], violating the invariant that authorization state changes must only occur when the authorizer intends them, causing scoped impact: liquidation failure or fund freeze (victim's operator is revoked at a critical moment, blocking a defensive action)? Proof idea: state-transition test; submit revocation during active operation window and assert downstream operation fails.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH] Can an unprivileged attacker replay an Authorization signature from EcrecoverAuthorizer on a different chain (after a hard fork that preserves the same verifyingContract address) under preconditions where the domain separator is computed dynamically using block.chainid (not cached), triggering call sequence: [attacker submits the original-chain signature on the forked chain where block.chainid differs], violating the invariant that signatures must not be valid across chains, causing scoped impact: gate bypass (authorization granted on forked chain without user consent)? Proof idea: fork simulation test; change block.chainid and assert old signature is rejected due to domain separator mismatch.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that the Permit2 PermitTransferFrom struct signed by the user does not bind to any Midnight market parameters (loanToken address, maturity, collateral list) by constructing a bundler call with a different market (different maturity, same loanToken) that still passes the Permit2 check, under preconditions where the user signed PermitTransferFrom(token=loanToken, amount=X, nonce=N, deadline=T) for one market, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with a different market struct but same loanToken and amount, using victim's permit], violating the invariant that a Permit2 signature must bind to the specific market operation the user intended, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption (user's tokens are used to fill offers in an unintended market)? Proof idea: unit test with two markets sharing loanToken; assert permit from market A cannot be used in market B call.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the absence of a witness binding in IPermit2.permitTransferFrom (the interface only exposes permitTransferFrom, not permitWitnessTransferFrom) to use a victim's Permit2 signature in a bundler call with different takes[] or collateralWithdrawals[] parameters than the user intended, under preconditions where the user signed PermitTransferFrom for MidnightBundles with amount=X, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral with victim's permit but substitutes malicious takes[] pointing to attacker-controlled offers], violating the invariant that a Permit2 signature must commit to the full operation parameters, causing scoped impact: direct loss (victim's tokens are used to fill attacker-crafted offers at unfavorable rates)? Proof idea: unit test substituting takes[] in bundler call with victim's permit; assert operation proceeds with wrong offers.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that pullToken is called with from=msg.sender (not taker) to construct a scenario where msg.sender != taker and the Permit2 signature is from msg.sender, allowing the attacker (as msg.sender) to pull their own tokens but credit the operation to a different taker, under preconditions where isAuthorized(taker, attacker) == true and attacker has signed a Permit2 permit for their own tokens, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral(taker=victim, permit=attacker's own permit)], violating the invariant that token pull and credit assignment must be consistent, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption (attacker's tokens are pulled but debt/credit is assigned to victim)? Proof idea: unit test with msg.sender != taker; assert credit goes to taker and tokens come from msg.sender.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker cause a nonce desync between the Permit2 nonce bitmap and the EcrecoverAuthorizer sequential nonce by submitting an EcrecoverAuthorizer authorization that grants the attacker operator rights, then using those rights to call a bundler function that consumes a Permit2 nonce on behalf of the victim, under preconditions where the attacker is authorized by the victim on Midnight and the victim has a pending Permit2 signature, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral(taker=victim, permit=victim's Permit2 sig) as authorized operator], violating the invariant that Permit2 nonce consumption must only occur with the token owner's explicit consent for the specific operation, causing scoped impact: unauthorized seizure (victim's Permit2 nonce is consumed and tokens pulled without victim initiating the bundler call)? Proof idea: multi-step test; authorize attacker, then attacker submits bundler call with victim's permit.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the deadline boundary condition (block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline, so equality is valid) to submit an Authorization exactly at the deadline block, under preconditions where the victim signed an Authorization with deadline=T and the attacker observes it at block.timestamp=T, triggering call sequence: [attacker submits Authorization at block.timestamp == deadline], violating the invariant that a deadline-expired authorization must not be submittable, causing scoped impact: gate bypass (authorization is granted in the last valid block, potentially after the user assumed it had expired)? Proof idea: fuzz deadline at boundary; assert setIsAuthorized succeeds when block.timestamp == deadline.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized does not check that the EcrecoverAuthorizer itself is authorized by the authorizer on Midnight before incrementing the nonce, causing the nonce to advance even when the downstream IMidnight.setIsAuthorized call will revert, under preconditions where the authorizer has NOT called midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, authorizer), triggering call sequence: [anyone calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, validSig); nonce increments; IMidnight.setIsAuthorized reverts; entire tx reverts], violating the invariant that nonce must only advance on successful authorization, causing scoped impact: fund freeze (nonce is NOT actually advanced due to revert, but this confirms the flow is safe — verify no partial state)? Proof idea: unit test calling setIsAuthorized without pre-authorizing EcrecoverAuthorizer; assert full revert with no nonce change.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the Permit2 unordered nonce bitmap to sign multiple permits with different nonces (e.g., nonce=0 and nonce=256, both in different word positions) and have both consumed in the same block, under preconditions where the user signed two PermitTransferFrom structs with nonce=0 and nonce=256 for the same token/amount, triggering call sequence: [two separate bundler calls in the same block each consuming one nonce], violating the invariant that a user should be able to control the ordering and consumption of their permits, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption (double-spend of token allowance across two bundler operations in the same block)? Proof idea: fuzz nonce pairs across different word positions; assert both can be consumed independently.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that the Permit2 PermitTransferFrom.permitted.amount is set to the bundler's amount parameter (not a user-specified max), meaning a user who signs for amount=X cannot be protected if the bundler is called with a different amount, under preconditions where the user signed PermitTransferFrom(amount=X) but the attacker calls the bundler with maxBuyerAssets=Y > X, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls buyWithUnitsTargetAndWithdrawCollateral(maxBuyerAssets=Y, permit with amount=X)], violating the invariant that Permit2 must enforce the signed amount ceiling, causing scoped impact: fund freeze (Permit2 reverts with InvalidAmount since requestedAmount=Y > permitted.amount=X, but this confirms the protection works — verify the check is correct)? Proof idea: unit test with mismatched amounts; assert Permit2 reverts with InvalidAmount.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that the Permit2 domain separator in the test uses EIP712Domain(string name,uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract) (with name='Permit2') while EcrecoverAuthorizer uses EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract) (no name), to craft a signature that is valid for one domain but submitted to the other, under preconditions where both contracts are deployed and the attacker has a valid signature for one, triggering call sequence: [attacker submits Permit2 signature to EcrecoverAuthorizer or vice versa], violating the invariant that domain separators must be distinct and non-interchangeable, causing scoped impact: gate bypass (cross-domain signature accepted)? Proof idea: differential test computing both domain separators; assert they are distinct and cross-submission reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that the Authorization struct's isAuthorized field is a bool (not an enum) and the AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH encodes it as bool, to craft a signature where isAuthorized=false (revocation) and submit it to revoke a victim's operator at a critical moment, under preconditions where the attacker has observed a signed revocation message in the mempool or obtained it through social engineering, triggering call sequence: [attacker submits revocation Authorization at block.timestamp when victim's operator is needed for a time-sensitive operation], violating the invariant that authorization revocations must only take effect when the authorizer submits them, causing scoped impact: liquidation failure (victim's defensive operator is revoked just before a liquidation they were trying to prevent)? Proof idea: time-sensitive state test; submit revocation during liquidation window and assert liquidation proceeds.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that MidnightBundles.pullToken constructs the PermitTransferFrom struct from the token address derived from market.collateralParams[collateralIndex].token (not from the permit data itself), meaning the token in the permit is always the market's collateral token, to submit a permit signed for a different token that happens to have the same address on a different chain or in a test environment, under preconditions where token addresses collide across deployments, triggering call sequence: [attacker calls supplyCollateralAndSellWithUnitsTarget with a permit signed for token A but the market uses token B at the same address], violating the invariant that Permit2 token binding must be unambiguous, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption (wrong token is pulled)? Proof idea: deploy two tokens at same address in different test forks; assert permit from one cannot be used for the other.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the sequential nonce in EcrecoverAuthorizer to perform a nonce exhaustion attack by submitting many valid Authorization messages (each incrementing the nonce) signed by an authorized delegate, under preconditions where the attacker is authorized by the victim (isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true) and can sign Authorization structs, triggering call sequence: [attacker signs and submits Authorization(authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true/false, nonce=0..N) repeatedly], violating the invariant that nonce exhaustion must not be possible by an authorized party, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption (victim's authorization state is toggled arbitrarily by attacker)? Proof idea: fuzz loop submitting N authorizations from authorized attacker; assert final isAuthorized state matches last submission.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IPermit2.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: pullToken / IPermit2.permitTransferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that the Permit2 signature covers spender=msg.sender (the bundler address, baked into the hash at signing time) to cause a signature signed for one bundler deployment to be unusable if the bundler is redeployed at a different address, under preconditions where MidnightBundles is redeployed (e.g., upgrade), triggering call sequence: [user signs permit for old bundler address; new bundler calls permitTransferFrom; Permit2 rejects because spender mismatch], violating the invariant that permit signatures must remain valid across bundler upgrades, causing scoped impact: fund freeze (all pre-signed permits become invalid after bundler redeployment)? Proof idea: deploy two bundler instances; assert permit signed for bundler A is rejected by bundler B.
